title: Internationalization and innovation of firms: evidence and policy
abstract: We use a representative and cross-country comparable sample of manufacturing firms (EFIGE) to document patterns of interaction among firm-level internationalization, innovation and productivity across seven European countries (Austria, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Spain, United Kingdom). We find strong evidence of positive association among the three firm-level characteristics across countries and sectors. We also find that the positive correlation between internationalization and innovation survives after controlling for productivity, with some evidence of causality running from the latter to the former. Our analysis suggests that export promotion per se is unlikely to lead to sustainable internationalization because internationalization goes beyond export and because, in the medium to long term, internationalization is likely driven by innovation. We recommend coordination and integration of internationalization and innovation policies ‘under one roof’ at both the national and EU levels, and propose a bigger coordinating role for EU institutions. — Carlo Altomonte, Tommaso Aquilante, Gábor Békés and Gianmarco I.P. Ottaviano
summary_en: Across seven European countries including Hungary, firms that export, innovate, and are productive tend to be the same firms. This paper uses the EFIGE survey of ~15,000 manufacturing firms to document these patterns and pushes further: controlling for productivity, internationalisation and innovation still reinforce each other, with some evidence that productivity gains drive later internationalisation. The authors conclude that export-promotion programmes that ignore innovation are unlikely to deliver lasting internationalisation, and argue for combining export and innovation policy tools in a single coordinated framework, ideally at the EU level.
data_used: EFIGE (European Firms in a Global Economy) cross-country survey of ~15,000 manufacturing firms with ≥10 employees in seven countries — Austria, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Spain, UK — fielded in 2010, with balance-sheet data from 2001–2009.
policy_relevance: Direct message for Hungarian Ministry of Economy: standalone export-promotion schemes (e.g., HEPA-type support) give weaker returns than programmes that combine export support with R&D and innovation subsidies. Concrete policy option is an integrated "one roof" support unit bundling HIPA/HEPA tools with innovation grants. Based on pre-2010 data; the rise of GVCs and nearshoring since may have shifted the balance further towards innovation.
